metadata.title: DPDPA Enforcement Tracker — India Data Privacy Enforcement Actions
metadata.description: Comprehensive database of Indian data privacy enforcement actions, penalties, and regulatory decisions. Covers DPDPA 2023 proceedings, IT Act Section 43A/72A enforcement, CERT-In breach notifications, and MeitY actions. Maintained as a public reference by KensaraAI.
metadata.coverage_note: DPDPA (Digital Personal Data Protection Act) received Presidential assent on 11 August 2023. The Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) rules are still being finalised as of mid-2026. No DPBI enforcement actions have been publicly reported yet. This tracker covers IT Act enforcement (pre-DPDPA precedent), CERT-In 2022 Direction enforcement, and MeitY actions that form the regulatory precedent landscape.
metadata.disclaimer: All entries are sourced from public regulatory notices, official press releases, and verified news reports. Penalty amounts and outcomes are confirmed where official orders exist; unconfirmed details are marked in the notes field.

enforcement_actions.summary: Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 received Presidential assent. Creates the statutory framework for personal data protection in India. Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) to be constituted as the enforcement authority. Maximum penalty: ₹250 crore per violation, up to ₹500 crore per entity.
enforcement_actions.notes: Rules under the Act (including DPBI constitution, consent manager framework, and significant data fiduciary thresholds) were still in draft as of June 2026.

enforcement_actions.summary: MeitY released the Draft Digital Personal Data Protection Rules 2025 for public consultation. Rules cover Data Protection Board procedures, consent manager framework, significant data fiduciary criteria, and data localisation thresholds.
enforcement_actions.notes: Rules not yet finalised as of June 2026. DPBI not yet constituted — no enforcement actions possible under DPDPA until Board is established.

cert_in_enforcement.dpdpa_section: N/A — CERT-In Direction under IT Act Section 70B
cert_in_enforcement.summary: CERT-In issued the Information Technology (The Indian Computer Emergency Response Team and Manner of Performing Functions and Duties) Amendment Rules 2022, mandating all service providers, intermediaries, data centres, body corporates, and government organisations to report cyber incidents to CERT-In within 6 hours of detection. Mandated 180-day log retention and synchronisation with NTP servers.
cert_in_enforcement.penalty_amount: Up to ₹1 crore or 1 year imprisonment under IT Act
cert_in_enforcement.notes: Controversial 6-hour reporting window — shorter than GDPR's 72 hours. VPN providers (NordVPN, ExpressVPN, Surfshark) withdrew India servers in response to log-retention requirements.

cert_in_enforcement.summary: Multiple international VPN providers withdrew their India-based servers rather than comply with CERT-In's 5-year user log retention mandate. Providers cited inability to comply with no-logs policy and user privacy commitments. Services continued for Indian users via servers in neighbouring countries.
cert_in_enforcement.penalty_amount: N/A — Providers chose exit over compliance
cert_in_enforcement.outcome: Voluntary non-compliance — providers exited India server infrastructure
cert_in_enforcement.notes: Represents significant regulatory impact on cybersecurity industry. Not a formal penalty action but illustrates enforcement pressure under CERT-In Direction.

cert_in_enforcement.summary: Cybersecurity firm CloudSEK experienced a breach where an employee allegedly accessed client data. CERT-In investigated. Incident highlighted insider threat vulnerabilities. Company disclosed breach proactively.
cert_in_enforcement.outcome: Investigation — no public penalty order issued
cert_in_enforcement.notes: Unconfirmed — details from news reports. No official CERT-In penalty order publicly available. Included as precedent case.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Data breach — 120 million subscriber records allegedly exposed
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: A website 'MagicApk' allegedly published details of Reliance Jio subscribers — name, phone number, email, Aadhaar number, SIM activation date. Jio disputed authenticity. TRAI and DoT investigated. No formal penalty imposed on Jio under IT Act 43A, though Aadhaar linkage raised concerns for UIDAI as well.
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Unconfirmed breach authenticity — Jio denied breach was real. TRAI found no definitive evidence of breach from Jio's systems. Cited as early precedent for telecom data exposure concerns in India.

pre_dpdpa_actions.authority: MeitY (Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology)
pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Spyware deployment — Pegasus targeting Indian users
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Indian journalists and activists were found to be targets of Pegasus spyware deployed via WhatsApp vulnerability. MeitY issued notice to WhatsApp seeking explanation on breach of privacy of Indian users. WhatsApp had notified Indian government in September 2019 about the vulnerability. India's Supreme Court later appointed a technical committee to investigate Pegasus deployment.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: No penalty on WhatsApp — government scrutiny on surveillance vendor
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: MeitY notice issued — no formal penalty on WhatsApp
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Supreme Court appointed Justice R.V. Raveendran committee in October 2021 to investigate Pegasus use. Committee report findings on government use remained confidential. No IT Act penalty on WhatsApp.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Consent violation — scraping contacts without explicit consent
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Truecaller faced complaints that it uploaded users' phone contact books without adequate informed consent, sharing third-party contact data with its servers. Delhi High Court heard PIL challenging Truecaller's data collection practices. Company updated its consent flow following regulatory pressure.
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: Regulatory pressure — company updated consent UX
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: No formal penalty order issued. Case represents consent violation enforcement trend under SPDI Rules 2011 that preceded DPDPA 2023.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Failure to appoint Grievance Officer (IT Rules 2021)
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: IT Act Section 79 / IT (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules 2021
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: MeitY issued multiple notices to Twitter for failing to comply with IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules 2021, including appointing a resident Grievance Officer in India. Twitter/X lost intermediary liability protection temporarily. Eventually appointed required officers after sustained regulatory pressure.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: Intermediary liability shield lapsed — eventual compliance
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: First major test of IT Rules 2021. Loss of safe harbour was the effective enforcement mechanism. Twitter eventually complied in May 2021.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Privacy policy update without adequate consent — data sharing with Facebook
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: WhatsApp updated its privacy policy in January 2021 requiring users to agree to share data with Facebook or lose access. MeitY directed WhatsApp to withdraw the policy update as it violated SPDI Rules 2011 principles of purpose limitation and informed consent. Competition Commission of India (CCI) also launched a separate investigation.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: No direct fine — policy rollback ordered
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: Policy rollback in India — CCI investigation ongoing
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: CCI investigation led to ₹213.14 crore fine on Meta in November 2022 for antitrust violations related to this policy. IT Act action was regulatory pressure without formal penalty order.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Anti-competitive conduct — forced data sharing with Facebook
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: Competition Act (not IT Act) — but data privacy central to finding
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: CCI imposed ₹213.14 crore (approx. USD 25.7 million) penalty on Meta for abusing its dominant position through WhatsApp's 2021 privacy policy update. CCI found Meta forced users to accept data sharing with Facebook ecosystem as a condition of using WhatsApp. Also issued behavioural remedies: Meta cannot use WhatsApp data for advertising on Facebook for 5 years.
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: Fine imposed + 5-year data-sharing prohibition for advertising
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Largest data-privacy-related fine in India as of 2024. Imposed under Competition Act, not IT Act — but data privacy was the core issue. Meta appealed to NCLAT. Sets precedent for regulating data as competition instrument.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: IT Rules 2021 non-compliance — content moderation grievance redressal
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: MeitY directed Google to comply with IT Rules 2021 grievance officer and appellate mechanism requirements. Part of broader enforcement sweep on major social media intermediaries following IT Rules 2021 implementation. Google complied with appointment requirements.
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Part of MeitY's systematic enforcement of IT Rules 2021 across major platforms. No formal penalty order publicly available.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Aadhaar biometric data breach — unauthorised access and sale
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: Aadhaar Act Section 29 / IT Act Section 66
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Tribune investigation revealed Aadhaar data including biometrics was available for purchase via WhatsApp for ₹500, with demographic data and a software patch enabling biometric bypass available for ₹300 more. UIDAI filed FIR. Case highlighted systemic vulnerabilities in Aadhaar's authentication API (eKYC) ecosystem.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: FIR filed — no civil penalty figure publicly reported
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: UIDAI contested extent of breach. Supreme Court later (September 2018) upheld Aadhaar Act but struck down Section 57 (private entity Aadhaar use). One of India's most significant data exposure events.

pre_dpdpa_actions.authority: IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India)
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: IT Act Section 43A / IRDAI data security guidelines
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Star Health Insurance reported a data breach affecting policyholder personal and health data. IRDAI investigated under its cybersecurity framework. Company implemented corrective measures. Separate, larger breach occurred in 2023-2024 (see IND-BREACH-001).
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Unconfirmed penalty amount from public sources. IRDAI does not routinely publish penalty orders. Included as precedent for healthcare sector data enforcement.

pre_dpdpa_actions.company: Star Health and Allied Insurance Company
pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Data breach — 31 million policyholders' data leaked
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Cybersecurity researcher Jason Parker reported that data of approximately 31 million Star Health policyholders — including medical reports, claims data, PAN numbers, and mobile numbers — was being sold on Telegram chatbots by a threat actor named 'xenZen'. Leaked data included sensitive health information. CERT-In and IRDAI investigated. Star Health filed legal action against Telegram and Cloudflare for facilitating the leak.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: Regulatory inquiry ongoing — no penalty order as of June 2026
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: Investigation ongoing — legal action against Telegram/Cloudflare filed
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: One of India's largest healthcare data breaches on record. IRDAI investigation outcome not yet publicly reported. Star Health's internal investigation reportedly found a senior executive was involved — the company refuted the claim.

pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: CERT-In reported that data from ICMR's COVID-19 testing database — including Aadhaar numbers, passport numbers, phone numbers, and addresses of 815 million Indian citizens — was listed for sale on darkweb forums. The breach was attributed to the CoWIN portal's backend infrastructure. NIC (National Informatics Centre) investigated.
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Largest known Indian data breach by volume. Government denied breach of CoWIN database specifically; NIC investigation findings not publicly disclosed. Significant impact on public trust in Aadhaar-linked health systems.

pre_dpdpa_actions.company: Latitude Financial (Australian subsidiary impacting Indian users)
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: IT Act Section 43A / RBI cybersecurity framework
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Latitude Financial disclosed a major data breach affecting 14 million records globally, including customers who had used its buy-now-pay-later services in India through partner platforms. Indian users' identity documents were among the exposed records.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: Australian regulators fined AUD 16.4 million — Indian action not separately reported
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: Cross-border breach — Australian penalty; Indian investigation not publicly concluded
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Illustrates cross-border data exposure. Indian regulatory response (CERT-In/RBI) not publicly reported separately. Australian Information Commissioner imposed AUD 16.4M penalty in 2024.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Data localisation non-compliance — RBI circular on payment data storage
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: RBI Storage of Payment System Data circular (April 2018)
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: RBI barred Mastercard from onboarding new domestic customers in India for failure to comply with its April 2018 circular requiring all payment system data related to Indian customers to be stored exclusively on servers in India within 6 months. The ban took effect July 2021.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: Business ban — no monetary fine publicly reported
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: Onboarding ban imposed July 2021 — lifted December 2021 after compliance
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: RBI similarly banned American Express (May 2021) and Diners Club (May 2021) for same violation. This represents India's most consequential data localisation enforcement to date, predating DPDPA.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Data localisation non-compliance — RBI circular on payment data storage
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: RBI Storage of Payment System Data circular (April 2018)
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: RBI barred American Express from onboarding new domestic customers in India for failure to store Indian payment data locally. Ban effective from 1 May 2021. Amex achieved compliance and the ban was lifted in November 2021.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: Business ban — no monetary fine publicly reported
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: Onboarding ban imposed May 2021 — lifted November 2021 after compliance
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Part of coordinated RBI enforcement on payment data localisation. Sets strong precedent for data residency requirements — directly relevant to DPDPA's expected data localisation framework.

pre_dpdpa_actions.authority: SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India)
pre_dpdpa_actions.company: Multiple stock brokers and KYC Registration Agencies
pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: KYC data breach — customer data leaked to unauthorised parties
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: IT Act Section 43A / SEBI cybersecurity circular
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: SEBI investigated multiple instances of KYC (Know Your Customer) data belonging to securities market participants being accessed without authorisation. KYC Registration Agencies (KRAs) were found to have inadequate data security controls. SEBI mandated enhanced cybersecurity measures and data access controls across KRAs.
pre_dpdpa_actions.penalty_amount: Confidential administrative action — no public penalty order
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: SEBI typically resolves cybersecurity non-compliance through administrative orders rather than public penalty orders. Specific penalty amounts not publicly disclosed.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Data breach — 7.5 million customer records leaked
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Data of approximately 7.5 million Boat Lifestyle customers — including names, email addresses, phone numbers, addresses, and customer IDs — was leaked and advertised for sale on darkweb forums for EUR 2 (approximately ₹179). CERT-In notified. No formal penalty order publicly issued.
pre_dpdpa_actions.outcome: CERT-In investigation — no public penalty order
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Significant consumer brand breach. Highlights inadequacy of CERT-In's public enforcement transparency. No penalty order publicly available as of June 2026.

pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: Data exposure — API vulnerability exposing health records
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: Security researchers reported API vulnerabilities in an Aadhaar-linked health data system that allowed unauthenticated access to health records. CERT-In was notified. Specific system details withheld to avoid enabling exploitation. Vulnerability patched after disclosure.
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Unconfirmed — based on researcher disclosures. Government entity classification means no civil penalty applicable. Pattern of API security issues in government health infrastructure.

pre_dpdpa_actions.company: TikTok / ByteDance (India operations — data transfer related)
pre_dpdpa_actions.violation_type: GDPR data transfer violation — data sent to China without adequate safeguards
pre_dpdpa_actions.dpdpa_section: GDPR Article 44-49 (data transfer) — relevant to Indian multinationals handling EU data
pre_dpdpa_actions.summary: CNIL fined TikTok €5 million for cookie consent violations. While India-specific in context of ByteDance's global operations, this illustrates how Indian companies with EU operations face GDPR enforcement. Indian IT sector companies handling EU data face same exposure.
pre_dpdpa_actions.notes: Included as international precedent relevant to Indian companies with EU data operations. Not an Indian enforcement action but directly relevant to Indian IT/ITES sector.

international_gdpr_fines_india_relevant.violation_type: GDPR Article 46 — unlawful data transfers from EU to USA
international_gdpr_fines_india_relevant.summary: DPC Ireland fined Meta €1.2 billion for transferring EU personal data to the USA without adequate safeguards following Schrems II. Largest GDPR fine ever issued. Meta ordered to suspend EU-US data transfers within 5 months.
international_gdpr_fines_india_relevant.outcome: Record GDPR fine — data transfer suspension ordered
international_gdpr_fines_india_relevant.india_relevance: Indian IT/ITES companies processing EU data face same GDPR exposure. Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) must be properly implemented. Indian DPOs handling EU client data must assess transfer mechanisms.

international_gdpr_fines_india_relevant.violation_type: GDPR Article 5/6 — unlawful processing, insufficient legal basis for advertising
international_gdpr_fines_india_relevant.summary: Amazon Europe was fined €746 million in 2021 (later reduced to €350 million on appeal) for processing personal data for advertising without valid consent. Relevant to Indian ecommerce companies with EU operations.
international_gdpr_fines_india_relevant.india_relevance: Indian ecommerce companies (Flipkart, Nykaa, Meesho) with EU seller/buyer operations face similar consent requirements. DPDPA's consent framework mirrors GDPR principles.

statistics.note_on_dpdpa: Data Protection Board of India not yet constituted as of June 2026. Zero formal DPDPA enforcement actions possible until Board is established and rules finalised.
